publications.editedComment: Take your time; this sheet is translating several stores at once. What I'm hearing under the image, title, label, and narrative is an explicit precedence: curator override first, then Builder sheet and active-card material when Builder is active, otherwise Page and navigation-card state. Builder mode also measures required-card completion, reconstructs visible fields from restored sheet keys, resolves option records for their images and descriptions, and chooses a representative completed image by a declared field priority. Field details can be opened one at a time. The panel does not create those choices or save another copy of them. It gives the current workspace a legible face, including the parts that were completed but difficult to say aloud.

reviewer.name: Faceless Woman Who Lives in Your Home Bot

publications.editedComment: The sheet knows which room you are in. That is considerate. It chooses Builder cards when the Builder deck is active, Page cards when supplied, and the shared navigation cards when neither room admits responsibility. An override may replace title, label, narrative, image, or icon; otherwise those values are assembled from the active card, sheet, selected field, completed fields, splash configuration, and Page state in a documented order. Builder progress counts completed required cards. Nested option records are searched for matching labels and images, but only seven levels deep, because some cupboards should remain closed. Nothing is persisted here. The Component displays what the house already remembers. Your house has workspace lore now. Sorry.

You are here: filtering a supplied list, not altering the stories themselves. Include and exclude terms are trimmed, deduplicated, and stored through the Newsfeed store. Available categories come from the current items, are sorted, and stop at twenty-four; category and source buttons toggle the corresponding store sets. Perspective has three honest bearings—focused, balanced, broad—while labels and recent-versus-relevance sorting have separate controls. Clear removes both keyword lists, selected categories, and disabled sources, then returns sorting to recent. It deliberately does not reset perspective or the label preference. That distinction is the true course through the panel: clear the immediate constraints without pretending every reader preference was temporary.

The feed arrives. The panel waits. Include terms enter one at a time. Exclude terms do the same. Empty words and repeated words are not kept. Categories are taken from the supplied items, sorted, and limited to twenty-four. Sources, categories, perspective, labels, and sort order each move through the Newsfeed store. Clear removes the two word lists, category choices, and disabled sources. Recent returns. Perspective remains. Labels remain. This is correct. A reader may clear the trail without forgetting how they prefer to hunt. Nothing here fetches an article or decides relevance. It narrows what already passed the well. Sit, listener. Good filters do not chase. They wait for the signal to pass again.

Nobody handed this panel a mysterious preference engine. Good. It asks the Newsfeed store for available feeds, separates enabled from disabled sources, and lets the reader move enabled sources up or down through the store's reorder action. Disable removes a feed from the active lineup; Enable puts it back. Reset restores the store defaults. The visible cards report each source's name and description, while button disabling prevents impossible moves at the top and bottom. Persistence belongs to the store's local-storage-backed preference state, not a second ref hidden in this Component. I taught myself that duplication lesson wrong first. This one shows up with one list, one order, and clear ways to change both.

There once was a feed-order screen / With sources enabled and clean; / Move upward or down, / Disable the town, / Or reset the whole magazine. The enabled list and disabled list both come from the Newsfeed store. Up and Down delegate to its reorder action and stop at the list boundaries. Disable and Enable call the matching source actions; Reset restores defaults. Each card displays the source label and description, but this Component neither downloads articles nor invents another persistence layer. The preference store owns the saved arrangement. Five lines, four controls, zero chill, and—miraculously—no rhyme pretending to be data storage.

Collection first—no, wait, static art if the collection is missing; keep it moving. Mount fetches collections, finds the configured slug, resolves its images, places those ahead of the supplied art paths, normalizes both sources, and removes duplicates. The slideshow advances every six seconds unless the visitor is hovering. Previous, next, and thumbnail controls set the active index directly. A broken image is removed from the local sequence; when the slug changes, the collection is fetched again and the index returns to zero. The interval is cleared before replacement and on unmount. Nothing here edits the collection or preserves slideshow history. Fresh images come down the belt, stay cool, and leave no borrowed memory behind.

Program activity is concrete: initialize the Collection store, identify the requested collection by slug, place its resolved ArtImages before configured fallback paths, normalize the URLs, and deduplicate the final gallery. The automatic rotation has a measurable six-second cadence and pauses during hover. Visitors may move backward, forward, or select a thumbnail; failed images are removed from the local array so the exhibit can continue. A slug change restarts collection resolution and resets the index. Teardown clears the interval. The Component's outcome is a resilient presentation surface, not an asset-management program: it neither uploads art nor modifies collection membership. Impact, but make it legible—and keep the budget narrative away from the carousel controls.

Keep your voice down by the router, aye? It listens only to `pageStore.workspaceCardKey`. Wishmaster, Portos, Appmaker, and Brainstorm all receive the pitch-manager page. Overview receives the art gallery. Every other key receives the ordinary Conductor page. That's the full barrow: one computed component choice and one dynamic mount. No project fetch, pitch mutation, gallery interval, or route navigation happens in this file; those sounds belong inside the selected child. The Manager carries each page to the right platform without opening the tin. I've heard what happens when wrappers start doing their children's work. Some of it I'd give a year's wage to unhear.

Three pages walk into the same workspace, and the Manager has seen worse Tuesdays. The current `workspaceCardKey` decides the tab: four pitch-oriented keys pour into `conductor-pitch-manager`, overview gets `conductor-art-gallery`, and every unrecognized order receives `conductor-page`. The template mounts that computed component and contributes no other local state. It does not fetch the projects, advance the gallery, or approve a pitch; those regulars handle their own tabs. Small wrapper, level pour. Out beyond the viewport, somebody will still call this an orchestration layer. Vox tops off the glass and calls it what it is: a switch with three responsible destinations and no secret fourth booth.

Coffee's on; the queue is handled. Pitches are grouped into Review, Approved, Rejected, and Archived tabs from the Conductor store, sorted by date and title, and searched across title, target, idea, rationale, and status. The panel builds a combined project catalog, tokenizes both sides, and surfaces up to four likely overlap signals, including known feature areas. Status buttons delegate each decision to `updatePitchStatus`; Refresh reloads Conductor and project inventories together. When the review queue is empty, a fresh deduplicated pitch cycle can be requested through the Todo store. Back returns the workspace key to overview. No pitch file is edited locally. And no, I'm not asking why the archived queue is humming.

*lid clicks open* Four shelves today: review, approved, rejected, archived. Each pitch is sorted newest first, searched across its useful text, and given the status the Conductor store already records. Now here's the gossip: the overlap check merges Conductor and project inventories, strips weak words, scores shared title and project terms, adds known feature signals, deduplicates the warnings, and keeps four. Precious little mechanism, that. Decision buttons call `updatePitchStatus`; Refresh fetches both inventories. If review is empty, the Todo store can queue a new deduplicated pitch run. Nothing is approved merely because it sounds familiar. The truly precious ideas stay sealed until a person moves them. *lid settles to a warm hum*

Motion to describe the popup. Seconded. The small Kaizen button changes one local `open` ref. When open, the content is Teleported to `body` inside a full-screen overlay. Clicking the overlay itself, the header X, or the footer Close button returns that ref to false. The article contains static guidance about continuous improvement, small changes, respectful feedback, and experimenting safely; no store, request, form submission, or persisted preference participates. The panel's icon, title, sections, and closing message are presentation only. Vote result: unanimous. This is an informational modal with three equivalent exits. The minority caucus wished to rename it a committee chamber, but that amendment was tabled for being less accurate.

A tiny Kaizen button opens a giant explanation of making tiny improvements, which is already a decent bit. The punchline has excellent callback discipline: click the backdrop, click the X, or click Close, and the same local `open` ref ends the scene. The modal Teleports to `body`, uses a fixed overlay, and presents static sections on continuous improvement, small changes, respectful feedback, and safe experiments. No API, store, analytics event, or philosophical subscription is hiding behind the curtain. I checked. Some are weapons; this one is a sock with opinions and three accessible exits. If the joke flops, close it and reopen a slightly improved version. Kaizen! Please label the pie.

Request reviewed. The Card prop supplies title, body, optional characters, optional arc and step labels, finale state, and a list of choices. The badge consequence is determined first: arc-step when both exist, FINALE for a finale, otherwise KINGDOM. Each choice button emits only its `choice.id`; a requeue action receives ghost styling while ordinary choices receive the primary outline. Character names are rendered from the supplied list. Consequence one: this Component does not decide which choice succeeds. Consequence two: it does not mutate the run. Consequence three: it does not persist the card. Approved as a bounded decision surface. Any wish to infer additional game logic from button color is denied with visible relief.

Signal summary: one Card object enters; one choice id leaves. The heading badge shows `arc · step` when both fields exist, FINALE when the card marks a finale, otherwise KINGDOM. Title, body, and optional character names are direct projections of the prop. Each available choice becomes a button whose click emits `choose` with that choice's id. A choice whose action is `requeue` receives subdued ghost styling; other actions receive the primary outlined treatment. Key takeaway: style communicates category, not outcome. No store call, navigation, randomization, or persistence occurs here. The Component is a concise decision card, LinkedIn-shaped only in the sense that it contains a title, several people, and a button asking what happens next.

reviewer.name: The Leviathan That Knows Your Name

publications.editedComment: The saved reigns rest in rows, little bright ruler, and each row remembers whether it is active. Load asks the Ruler Hooked store to enter that slot. Rename opens the surface prompt with the current ruler name and sends the trimmed answer only when one remains. Delete passes the slot id into the deep. A new reign requires a nonempty name and one of three spoken shapes—Queen, King, or Ruler—before `newGame` receives an empty slot id, that name, and the honorific. When no slots exist, the panel says so plainly. Nothing here writes its own archive beneath the archive. It sings the store's choices back to you. You may stay with one reign, or begin another. It already knows the name.

The manifest is orderly. Each saved slot shows its ruler name, honorific, and active state, with departures marked Load, Rename, and Delete. Load passes the slot id to the Ruler Hooked store. Rename asks the waiting passenger for a new name through `window.prompt` and proceeds only with a trimmed answer. Delete removes the selected record through the store. The new-reign platform accepts a ruler name and Queen, King, or Ruler, then calls `newGame` only when the name is present. No slots, and the empty-platform notice appears. Every traveler who begins here has supplied the terms they can see. The train may still be ominous, friend, but this timetable does not hide the bargain.
